rationale: The cited paper is used in the conceptual framing and discussion as prior evidence that strong self-brand connection consumers defend brands when faced with potentially negative brand-use information. The citing paper then positions its own contribution as showing a different defensive outcome for strong-SBC consumers: upgrading to exclusive products when dissimilar users are present. Even though the citation appears with other studies, it plays a clear role in motivating and interpreting the paper's SBC-based contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Ferraro et al.'s SBC defense logic from conspicuous-usage dilution effects to a new threat context (dissimilar brand users) and a different response variable (upgrading behavior). It uses the cited finding as a springboard to argue that strong-SBC consumers do more than preserve attitudes; they also take status-seeking purchase actions.
